text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

For all the pomp and ceremony, it is hard to ignore the blatant hypocrisy

PARIS —Paris, city of ideas, romance, ambition, renewal, hope, light, of course, and, for the third time,the Olympic Games. What could be more va-va-voom than a parade of athletes on the Seine?

The competitors delivered to the opening ceremony by boat, disembarking at the entrance to the Trocadero Gardens across the Pont d’Lena from the Eiffel Tower.

TakeThatWorld could be the newwhat3wordsfor Paris, as brazen a dose of self-absorption as you could ever wish to see.

They might also have lined the Champs-Elysees with paintings by Monet and Cezanne, representations of Victor Hugo and Honore de Balzac, photos of Mr and Mrs 1960s Alain Delon and Brigitte Bardot, pictures ofZinedine Zidaneand Suzanne Lenglen and any other emblems of French cultural life down the ages that tickled the fancy ofEmmanuel Macron, a president very much at the centre of his own Belle Epoque.

The show opened with an oh-so stylish video featuring Zizou carrying the Olympic torch through Paris before passing the flame to a small group of children, who emerged by some quirk of design upon the Seine to light up the bridge at Austerlitz and set the first Olympic barge on its way.

The Olympicshave always been a shameless propaganda tool for hosts, hijacked by bombasts and nation builders. Sport for all? Depends on your pocket.

Tickets for the opening ceremony were changing hands for the best part of €3,000. High-end hospitality at the men’s 100m final retail at €10,200. Perhaps the pricing was a factor in more than a million tickets remaining unsold at the point the Olympic flame was lit.

Sharp-eyed commentators have noted how this global pageant means something else entirely for President Macron and the International Olympic Committee (IOC) than it does for rank and file Parisians who find little to connect their lives in the banlieues with the games. This is an event entirely facing outward.

In a withering critique inInternational Politik Quarterly,Nabila Ramdani highlights the association with the Games of corporate and industrial power brokers, many of whom are helping foot the projected €8.9bn bill.

For them the coming two weeks are an extravaganza of impression management.

Chaumet, just one of the luxury goods brands owned by theworld’s richest man, Bernard Arnault, under the umbrella company LVMH, has designed the Olympic medals. Another of his blue chip brands, Moet Hennessy, is the bubbly served across the hospitality sector. “It is only natural that LVMH and its houses would be part of this exceptional international event,” cooed Arnault.

Cynics might come to the same conclusion as Ramdani, that none of this is aimed at nor is accessible to the melting pot of suburban dwellers. Rather it targets the allies, partners, investors and conglomerates that drive the machinery of global exchange. And this in the city that thrust the dispossessed to the forefront of history during the French Revolution more than 200 years ago.

Those oldsans-culottesmust be rushing to the celestial barricades. All those heads removed, all that bloody terror in the name of “liberte, egalite, fraternite”, only for the same social and economic barriers to re-emerge.

Never mind, Ramdani says, at least the Olympic mascots are on point in their red Phrygian caps favoured by the revolutionaries. And both the men’s and women’s time trials pass through the Bastille. Top end tokenism, there.

Watching all this unfold on a platform a few metres from Macron, sundry heads of state, and the red carpet beau monde, you wondered where all this might go next? What might be the logical extension of an opening ceremony after Paris, which felt more like an end point than a beginning.

Who has €8.9bn to burn on a glorified ad campaign? Is anything worth the stress of shutting down the most visited city on earth for days, of alienating people who live in the place, forcing upon them the necessary disruption and inconvenience?

And that’s before you get to the potential for random catastrophes, ambush by protesters, and acts of God that can bring any host nation low.

On the morning of the opening ceremony the organisers woke tosignal boxes ablazealong France’s high-speed TGV train network, timed to cause maximum disruption.

The British athletes announced they were done withthe Olympic Village food offeringbecause of shortages and uncooked meat.

Eggs, chicken and certain carbohydrate staples are in short supply according toTeam GBchief executive Andy Anson.

No matter, the day began with the Olympic Torch in the hands of Snoop Dogg in another nod towards the positioning of the Games as a red-carpet event. This show always goes on.

A total of 44,000 metal barriers fenced off the Seine along the 6km route travelled by the athletes from  their starting point at Pont d’Austerlitz to the Trocadero Gardens where the important people awaited their arrival.

As many as 300,000 were expected to line the route, waving mini tricolours and the like, just as the millions across 450 towns and cities had done since the torch landed in France on 8 May.

Its final journey was another odyssey of epic choreography, beginning in the capital 12 days ago.

A boat carrying the refugee team was the next to set sail, garnering a huge round of applause from the VIP seats.

In a different setting not too far from here,refugees attempting a water crossing in small vesselsevinces an entirely different response.

Perhaps the poor souls in Calais should paint five rings on the side of their boats to ensure safe passage and a warm welcome.

TheSaudi Arabiacontingent was also well received, perhaps pointing towards a future where the Games are held permanently in the kingdom in temperature-controlled stadiums newly built every four years.

Prince Mohammed bin Salman, the de facto head of state, has targeted 2036 as the moment to host, perhaps in the futuristic metropolis currently under construction in the desert,Neom.

By then Saudi Arabia might be the only country that can afford it, or rather consider it value for money.

Whatever delights the crown prince commissions for his opening ceremony, it will never be Paris, at least not with Lady Gaga vibing her best Marilyn Monroe on the banks of the Seine. And yes, Macron loved it.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: The Paris 2024 Olympics is fooling no-one with its shameless propaganda
author: Kevin Garside
description: For all the pomp and ceremony, it is hard to ignore the blatant hypocrisy
